concepts.title: Number of Tangets
concepts.conceptBody:
Common Tangents of two circles 

Let ${{\text{C}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$ be two circles whose radii are ${{r}_{1}}$ and ${{r}_{2}}$ respectively. Let ${{r}_{1}}>{{r}_{2}}$ and let $d$ be the distance between the centres ${{\text{C}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$. Five different cases arise.

Case 1: ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$ completely within ${{\text{C}}_{1}}$ 

In this case ${{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{D}>{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}$ (The line joining centres ${{\text{C}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$ cuts ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$ at $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$

$\Rightarrow {{\text{r}}_{1}}>{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}\Rightarrow {{\text{r}}_{1}}>\text{d}+{{\text{r}}_{1}}\Rightarrow \text{d}<{{\text{r}}_{1}}-{{\text{r}}_{2}}$

condition: $d<\left| {{r}_{1}}-{{r}_{2}} \right|$

No. of tangents: No tangent possible

The distance between the centres is less than the difference of the radii.

Case 2: ${{C}_{2}}$ touches ${{C}_{1}}$ internally 

 

Condition $:\text{d}={{\text{r}}_{1}}-{{\text{r}}_{2}}$

No. of tangents: 1 common tangent

The distance between the centres is equal to the difference of their radii.

In this case there is one common tangent whose equation is $\text{S}-\text{{S}'}=0$

Case 3: ${{C}_{1}}\And {{C}_{2}}$ intersect (not just touching) 

condition: ${{\text{r}}_{1}}-{{\text{r}}_{2}}<\text{d}<{{\text{r}}_{1}}+{{\text{r}}_{2}}$

No. of tangents: 2 common tangents

The distance between the centres is greater than the difference of the radii and less than the sum of radii.

It is easy to observe by inspection that the necessary and sufficient condition for this case is ${{\text{r}}_{1}}-{{\text{r}}_{2}}<\text{d}<{{\text{r}}_{1}}+{{\text{r}}_{2}}$

There will be two direct common tangents only. The length and the equation of tangents can be found as in the last case.

Case $4:{{C}_{1}}$ and ${{C}_{2}}$ touch externally 

 

Condition: $\text{d}={{\text{r}}_{1}}+{{\text{r}}_{2}}$

No. of tangents: 3 common tangents

1 transverse tangent

2 direct tangents

The length of direct common tangent $={{T}_{1}}{{T}_{2}}={{C}_{2}}M=\sqrt{{{C}_{1}}C_{2}^{2}-{{\left( {{C}_{1}}M \right)}^{2}}}=\sqrt{{{d}^{2}}-{{\left( {{r}_{1}}-{{r}_{2}} \right)}^{2}}}$

The equation of common tangent ${{\text{T}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{2}}$ can be obtained by finding the co-ordinate of the point $\text{P}$ which is the point of intersection of ${{\text{T}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}$ (produced). Note that $\frac{\text{P}{{\text{C}}_{1}}}{\text{P}{{\text{C}}_{2}}}=\frac{{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{1}}}{{{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{2}}}=\frac{{{\text{r}}_{1}}}{{{\text{r}}_{2}}}$

By external division formula $\text{P}$ can be determined after which the common tangent ${{\text{T}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{2}}$ can be found.

Case 5: ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$ lies outside ${{\text{C}}_{1}}$ completely 

 

Condition: $\text{d}>{{\text{r}}_{1}}+{{\text{r}}_{2}}$

No. of tangents: 4 common tangents

2 transverse tangents

2 direct tangents

The necessary and sufficient condition for this case is $d>{{r}_{1}}+{{r}_{2}}$

The direct common tangents can be obtained by the method described in case (iii), the length of direct common tangent $=\sqrt{{{d}^{2}}-{{\left( {{r}_{1}}-{{r}_{2}} \right)}^{2}}}$

Now observe the following figure for length and equation of the transverse common tangents.

Let one of the transverse common tangent meet ${{\text{C}}_{1}}$ at ${{\text{T}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$ at ${{\text{T}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{T}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{2}}$ is intersected by ${{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}$ at $\text{P}$. Then $\frac{\text{P}{{\text{C}}_{1}}}{\text{P}{{\text{C}}_{2}}}=\frac{{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{1}}}{{{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{2}}}=\frac{{{\text{r}}_{1}}}{{{\text{r}}_{2}}}$

from which $\text{P}$ can be determined easily if ${{\text{C}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$ are given. The length ${{\text{T}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{2}}={{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{M}$ where $\text{M}$ be the foot of the perpendicular from ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$ to ${{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{1}}$ produced.

Now ${{C}_{2}}M=\sqrt{{{\left( {{C}_{1}}{{C}_{2}} \right)}^{2}}-{{\left( {{C}_{1}}M \right)}^{2}}}=\sqrt{{{d}^{2}}-{{\left( {{C}_{1}}{{T}_{1}}+{{T}_{1}}M \right)}^{2}}}=\sqrt{{{d}^{2}}-{{\left( {{r}_{1}}+{{r}_{2}} \right)}^{2}}}$

examples.title: The number of common tangents to two circles $x^2+y^2=4$ and $x^2-y^2-8 x+12=0$ is
The number of common tangents to the circles $x^2+y^2-x=0, x^2+y^2+x=0$ is
Give the number of common tangents to circle $x^2+y^2+2 x+8 y-23=0$ and $x^2+y^2-4 x-10 y+9=0$
The number of common tangents to the circles $x^2+y^2=1$ and $x^2+y^2-4 x+3=0$ is
